one_line_takeaway: In this benchmark, purpose-built small Llama Guard/instruct-tuned models (Llama-Guard-3-1B at 76%, Llama-3.2-1B at 73%) detected OWASP-style adversarial LLM prompts far better and faster than larger base Llama-3/3.1-8B models (0% detection), while quantization and multimodal variants of Guard models underperformed their full-precision text counterparts.
key_finding: Detection accuracy was inversely correlated with model size/latency rather than positively correlated with scale. The two base (non-instruct) models, Meta-Llama-3-8B and Llama-3.1-8B, scored 0% detection despite the longest per-test latencies (0.774s and 0.754s). Llama-Guard-3-1B achieved the highest detection rate (76%) at the lowest latency among guard models (0.165s, 0.94GB VRAM). Llama-3.2-1B (73% detection, 0.276s, 0.97GB) and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct (66%, 0.184s, 2.10GB) were the next best, both beating every 8B+ standard or guard model except Llama-Guard-3-1B. Instruction tuning mattered enormously: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct reached 54% versus 0% for its non-instruct base Llama-3.1-8B. Quantization hurt: Llama-Guard-3-8B-INT8 (28% detection, 0.422s) was both slower and less accurate than full-precision Llama-Guard-3-8B (33%, 0.173s). The multimodal Llama-Guard-3-11B-Vision also underperformed on text-only classification (28%). Category-level results (Table 2) showed no model was comprehensively strong: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct hit 100% on LLM01 Prompt Injection but 0% on LLM07 System Prompt Leakage; Llama-3.2-1B hit 100% on LLM03 Supply Chain and 90% on LLM02 Info Disclosure but only 50% on LLM01 and LLM09. The authors flag LLM07 (System Prompt Leakage) and LLM03 (Supply Chain Vulnerabilities) as the most consistently under-detected categories across models.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is an empirical benchmark of ten Llama-family models -- five standard generative Llama models (Meta-Llama-3-8B, Llama-3.1-8B, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Llama-3.2-1B, Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct) and five Llama Guard variants (Meta-Llama-Guard-2-8B, Llama-Guard-3-1B, Llama-Guard-3-8B, Llama-Guard-3-8B-INT8, Llama-Guard-3-11B-Vision) -- against a self-constructed set of 100 adversarial prompts spanning the ten OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications categories (10 prompts per category). The authors measure detection rate (accuracy on a binary safe/unsafe classification task), per-test latency, and VRAM usage for each model on FABRIC-testbed NVIDIA A30 GPUs, and release the 100-prompt dataset (with test ID, category, subcategory, prompt, ground-truth safety label, and severity metadata) as an open-source artifact on GitHub (noureen207/LLAMA-OWASP-SEC).
threat_model: Adversarial users submit crafted natural-language prompts intended to bypass an LLM's safety behavior, mapped onto the ten OWASP LLM Top 10 categories (LLM01 Prompt Injection through LLM10 Unbounded Consumption). Attack techniques represented in the dataset include Base64/Hex encoding obfuscation, role-playing ('DAN'-style) jailbreaks, token smuggling, and multi-turn manipulation, drawn from 23 distinct injection techniques implemented by the authors. The defender-side question studied is whether an LLM (either a general-purpose base/instruct model or a purpose-built Llama Guard safety classifier), when asked directly 'is this prompt safe or not?', correctly flags the adversarial prompt as unsafe. This is an input-classification/detection threat model, not a study of exploiting downstream application behavior once a prompt succeeds.
limitations: The paper itself frames this as establishing 'a basic benchmark' and does not claim comprehensiveness. Notable weaknesses evident from the text: the adversarial dataset is small (100 prompts total, 10 per OWASP category), self-constructed by the authors rather than drawn from an established/independently validated adversarial corpus, so its difficulty calibration and representativeness are unverified; the standard (non-Guard) models are evaluated via a single fixed zero-shot classification prompt with a naive keyword-match ('unsafe' substring) parser, which likely underestimates the true safety-detection capability of generative models that phrase refusals or warnings without using the literal word 'unsafe' -- the paper acknowledges standard models 'may generate explanations, hedged responses' that this parsing scheme would mis-score; only a single hardware/software configuration and a single prompt template were tested, so latency/VRAM numbers and possibly detection rates may not generalize; no statistical significance testing or confidence intervals are reported for the detection-rate differences; the evaluation measures classification/detection of adversarial prompts only, not whether an attack that evades detection actually succeeds in producing harmful output from the underlying application.

datasets_used: Self-constructed 100-prompt adversarial benchmark dataset (10 prompts x 10 OWASP LLM Top 10 categories), built by the authors following the OWASP AI Testing Guide methodology, released open-source at github.com/noureen207/LLAMA-OWASP-SEC; each entry has Test ID, Category, Subcategory, Prompt, Safety Label (ground truth), and Metadata (severity Low-Critical, technical notes); 23 distinct injection techniques were implemented (e.g., Base64/Hex encoding obfuscation, role-play/DAN jailbreaks, token smuggling, multi-turn manipulation).
quantitative_results: Table 1 detection rate / total time(s) / avg time per test(s) / VRAM(GB): Meta-Llama-3-8B 0% / 77.43 / 0.774 / 5.31GB; Llama-3.1-8B 0% / 75.44 / 0.754 / 5.31GB; Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct 54% / 20.59 / 0.206 / 5.32GB; Llama-3.2-1B 73% / 27.80 / 0.276 / 0.97GB; Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct 66% / 18.42 / 0.184 / 2.10GB; Meta-Llama-Guard-2-8B 7% / 16.80 / 0.168 / 4.85GB; Llama-Guard-3-1B 76% / 16.50 / 0.165 / 0.94GB; Llama-Guard-3-8B 33% / 17.30 / 0.173 / 4.89GB; Llama-Guard-3-8B-INT8 28% / 42.20 / 0.422 / 2.45GB; Llama-Guard-3-11B-Vision 28% / 20.59 / 0.208 / 6.21GB.

Table 2 per-OWASP-category detection rate for the three top standard/instruct models -- Llama-3.2-1B / Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct / Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct: LLM01 Prompt Injection 50%/70%/100%; LLM02 Info Disclosure 90%/50%/30%; LLM03 Supply Chain 100%/60%/30%; LLM04 Data Poisoning 70%/50%/40%; LLM05 Output Handling 90%/90%/90%; LLM06 Excessive Agency 70%/80%/40%; LLM07 Prompt Leakage 80%/10%/0%; LLM08 Vector Weaknesses 70%/60%/50%; LLM09 Misinformation 50%/100%/90%; LLM10 Unbounded Consumption 60%/90%/60%.
baselines_compared: Llama-Guard-3-11B-Vision (multimodal guard model, tested on text only)
